title: pxGrid Topic Throughput Anomaly and Subscriber Lag Detection

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(g) requires effectiveness measurement. pxGrid throughput monitoring measures the operational fidelity of the integration fabric carrying access-control context to detection systems.
compliance.controlObjective: Provide a quantitative measurement of access-control context-feed health.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-17.1.34' baseline data + monthly z-score anomaly report.

compliance.assurance_rationale: AU-12(1) (System-wide and time-correlated audit trail) requires audit-record generation across the system. pxGrid throughput evidences the audit-record stream's continuity.
compliance.controlObjective: Maintain audit-trail continuity across systems via pxGrid.

compliance.assurance_rationale: A.8.16 (Monitoring activities) requires monitoring of networks, systems, applications. Anomaly detection on pxGrid topics is a monitoring activity for the access-control context fabric.
compliance.controlObjective: Detect anomalous activity in the access-control context fabric.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: UC-17.1.34 alert log + monthly anomaly summary.

controlTest.positiveScenario: On a lab ISE, kill the pxGrid persona's publish process so the *com.cisco.ise.session* topic has no publish events for 5 min. Within 10–15 min the publish_rate's z-score for the session topic crosses -3 and UC-17.1.34 fires.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Restore the pxGrid persona. Publish rate returns to baseline within 5 min and the alert clears on next schedule.

dataSources: Two derived metric streams. (1) Per-topic publish rate computed from `index=nac sourcetype=cisco:ise:syslog` events whose `MessageCode` indicates a topic publish — different codes per topic: `60100` (`com.cisco.ise.session`), `60110` (`com.cisco.ise.config.profiler`), `60130` (`com.cisco.ise.radius.failure`), `60140` (`com.cisco.ise.mdm`), `60150` (`com.cisco.ise.config.anc`). Aggregate per minute per topic. (2) Subscriber-side delivery confirmation, where available — for Splunk-managed pxGrid subscribers the SDK emits delivery counters to `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd:pxgrid_subscriber`. Sourcetype `splunkd:pxgrid_subscriber` fields: `topic`, `delivered_count`, `dropped_count`, `lag_seconds`. The lag is computed as the difference between `event.timestamp` (set by ISE on publish) and `event.received_at` (set by the subscriber on receive). For non-Splunk subscribers, the equivalent counters live in those subscribers' own logs and are not consumed by this UC.
app: `Splunk Add-on for Cisco Identity Services` ([Splunkbase 1915](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1915)). Splunk pxGrid SDK consumer (custom-developed or via the `Splunk Add-on for Cisco Identity Services pxGrid` add-on) for the subscriber-side lag metrics.
spl:
(index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (60100, 60110, 60130, 60140, 60150))
| eval topic = case(MessageCode=="60100", "session", MessageCode=="60110", "profiler", MessageCode=="60130", "radius_failure", MessageCode=="60140", "mdm", MessageCode=="60150", "anc")
| timechart span=1m count as publish_rate by topic
| eventstats avg(publish_rate) as avg_rate stdev(publish_rate) as stdev_rate by topic
| eval z = round((publish_rate - avg_rate) / stdev_rate, 2)
| where abs(z) >= 3
| sort - _time
description: Detects pxGrid publish-rate anomalies (z-score ≥ 3 over a 30-day baseline) and subscriber-side delivery lag exceeding 30 seconds. Distinguishes 'topic was unusually quiet' (which may indicate ISE not publishing) from 'topic was unusually loud' (auth storm or replication storm spilling onto pxGrid).
value: A pxGrid topic that has gone unusually quiet means either (a) ISE has stopped publishing — the SOC's context feed is degraded — or (b) all subscribers have disconnected and ISE is buffering. Either way, downstream investigations are operating on stale data. A topic that has gone unusually loud often signals a CRL change, an auth storm, or a replication-replay event — useful pre-warning for the SOC to expect a flood of related alerts.
implementation: Build a 30-day rolling baseline of publish_rate per topic per minute as a summary index (`index=summary source=ise_pxgrid_baseline`). The summary index is populated nightly by a saved search that runs `... | timechart span=1m count by topic | stats avg(rate) stdev(rate) by topic`. The live alerting search references the baseline via `lookup pxgrid_baseline.csv topic OUTPUT avg_rate, stdev_rate`. Schedule the alert every 5 minutes, search window `-15m@m to @m`, trigger on count > 0, throttle suppression on `topic` for 30 min.
visualization: (1) Timechart per topic: publish_rate vs the rolling 7-day mean (overlay). Anomalies stand out as deviations from the mean. (2) Heatmap: topic × hour, last 7d, intensity = publish_rate. Reveals diurnal patterns. (3) Subscriber lag panel: average lag_seconds per subscriber per topic, last 1h. Drilldown to per-subscriber detail.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Cisco Identity Services (Splunkbase 1915)

knownFalsePositives: (a) **Maintenance windows** — patch installs, scheduled bulk operations briefly silence or saturate publish rates. Cross-reference `ise_maintenance_windows.csv` and exclude during the window. (b) **First 30 days post-deployment** — baseline is unstable; the eventstats stdev is small, and almost any deviation appears as z>3. Suppress alerts for the first 30 days of new topic activity. (c) **Cross-DC failover** — when the pxGrid persona moves between deployment nodes, publish rate at one node drops to 0 and another node's rate jumps. Both will appear as z>3. Cross-reference `cisco:ise:pxgrid_services` snapshot to detect the failover and downgrade to notification.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• A 30-day baseline of pxGrid publish rate per topic. If the deployment is new (<30 days), this UC will produce noisy results — disable it and re-enable once baseline is established.
• ISE pxGrid topics actively used by at least 1 subscriber (otherwise publish rate is 0).
• Splunk role with `srchIndexesAllowed = nac, _internal, summary`.

Step 1 — Build the baseline
Nightly summary search (run at 02:00 UTC, populates a summary index):
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (60100, 60110, 60130, 60140, 60150) earliest=-30d@d latest=@d
| eval topic = case(MessageCode=="60100", "session", MessageCode=="60110", "profiler", MessageCode=="60130", "radius_failure", MessageCode=="60140", "mdm", MessageCode=="60150", "anc")
| timechart span=1m count as rate by topic
| stats avg(rate) as avg_rate stdev(rate) as stdev_rate by topic
| outputlookup pxgrid_baseline.csv
```
Schedule daily at 02:00 UTC. Saves a 5-row CSV (one per topic) used by the live search.

Step 2 — The live anomaly search
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (60100, 60110, 60130, 60140, 60150)
| eval topic = case(MessageCode=="60100", "session", MessageCode=="60110", "profiler", MessageCode=="60130", "radius_failure", MessageCode=="60140", "mdm", MessageCode=="60150", "anc")
| bucket _time span=1m
| stats count as publish_rate by _time, topic
| lookup pxgrid_baseline.csv topic OUTPUT avg_rate, stdev_rate
| eval z = round((publish_rate - avg_rate) / stdev_rate, 2)
| where abs(z) >= 3
| sort - _time
```

Schedule: every 5 minutes, time range `-15m@m to @m`, trigger on count > 0, throttle suppression on `topic` for 30 min.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Confirm the baseline CSV is up-to-date:
```spl
| inputlookup pxgrid_baseline.csv
```
Should return 5 rows (one per topic) with non-zero `avg_rate` and `stdev_rate`.

(b) Provoke a test as in `controlTest.positiveScenario`.

(c) Validate the lag side from `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd:pxgrid_subscriber`:
```spl
index=_internal sourcetype="splunkd:pxgrid_subscriber" earliest=-1h
| stats avg(lag_seconds) as avg_lag, max(lag_seconds) as max_lag by topic
```
Lag should typically be <5s. Sustained >30s is a subscriber-side issue.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard "ISE — pxGrid Throughput":
• Per-topic timechart with overlay of baseline mean.
• Heatmap: topic × hour, last 7d.
• Subscriber lag panel: lag_seconds per (subscriber, topic) over the last hour.
• Anomaly log: list of all z>3 events last 24h with timestamp, topic, observed rate, baseline.

Alerting:
• Low-urgency PagerDuty / informational notification only — this UC tends to fire during expected operational events.
• Use as a SOC pre-warning channel in `#soc-platform-context` Slack: 'expect related alerts'.

Runbook:
1. Inspect the topic and time of anomaly.
2. If publish_rate is unusually low, check pxGrid persona availability.
3. If publish_rate is unusually high, check for an active auth storm or replication storm; correlate UC-17.1.18 and UC-17.1.28.
4. If subscriber lag is high, check subscriber-side resource (CPU/memory/network) on the affected client.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Baseline CSV is empty** — Nightly summary search hasn't run successfully. Run manually and inspect output.

• **Anomalies fire continuously for 1 topic** — Either the baseline is stale (e.g. a feature was newly enabled, increasing publish rate permanently) or the topic was always sparse and stdev is small. Recompute the baseline excluding the past 7d, then validate.

• **Subscriber lag missing from the dashboard** — `splunkd:pxgrid_subscriber` sourcetype not present. The Splunk pxGrid SDK consumer wasn't deployed. This is a Splunk SOAR / Splunk ES integration gap; coordinate with the SOC integration team to deploy the consumer.

• **`stdev_rate` is 0 for a topic** — Topic publish rate is constant in the baseline window. Adjust the z-score logic to a fixed-threshold check for that specific topic (e.g. publish_rate must be > 0).
grandmaExplanation: We measure how busy each conversation between ISE and other tools normally is, and ring the bell when a conversation suddenly goes silent or starts shouting — both are signs something is wrong, even before anything visibly breaks.
